text: Overtraining syndrome is primarily related to sustained high training load, often coupled with other life stressors.
Monotonous training (low day-to-day variability in training load) increases the likelihood of developing overtraining syndrome.
Training monotony can be quantified as the daily mean training load divided by its standard deviation, where higher values indicate less day-to-day variation.
Training strain, calculated as training load multiplied by training monotony, is a strong predictor of illness incidence in athletes.
Session RPE multiplied by session duration provides a practical method for quantifying internal training load without heart rate monitors.
A high percentage of illnesses in athletes can be accounted for when individually identifiable training strain thresholds are exceeded.
Training thresholds associated with illness are individually variable, requiring personalized monitoring rather than universal cutoffs.
